01-01: 1983: The Internet switches to the TCP/IP protocol, marking the birth of the modern internet.
01-02: 1492: The Reconquista ends as Granada, the last Moorish emirate in Spain, surrenders to Catholic monarchs.
01-03: 1959: Alaska is officially admitted as the 49th state of the United States.
01-04: 2010: The Burj Khalifa, the world's tallest building, officially opens in Dubai.
01-05: 1914: Ford Motor Company introduces a minimum wage of $5 a day and an eight-hour workday.
01-06: 2021: A crowd of supporters of US President Donald Trump storms the United States Capitol.
01-07: 1610: Galileo Galilei first observes the four largest moons of Jupiter through his telescope.
01-08: 1918: US President Woodrow Wilson outlines his Fourteen Points for peace after World War I.
01-09: 2007: Apple CEO Steve Jobs introduces the original iPhone at the Macworld convention.
01-10: 1920: The League of Nations holds its first official meeting, ratifying the Treaty of Versailles.
01-11: 1922: Insulin is administered to a human patient for the first time to treat diabetes.
01-12: 1908: A wireless radio message is successfully transmitted from the Eiffel Tower for the first time.
01-13: 1898: French writer Émile Zola publishes his famous open letter 'J'Accuse…!' regarding the Dreyfus affair.
01-14: 1784: The United States ratifies the Treaty of Paris, formally ending the American Revolutionary War.
01-15: 2001: Wikipedia, a free and collaborative online encyclopedia, is launched.
01-16: 1920: Prohibition begins in the United States as the 18th Amendment to the Constitution takes effect.
01-17: 1991: Operation Desert Storm begins with massive coalition airstrikes against Iraqi forces.
01-18: 1919: The Paris Peace Conference opens to negotiate peace treaties following World War I.
01-19: 1915: Georges Claude is granted a patent for the neon discharge tube for use in advertising.
01-20: 1981: Iran releases 52 American hostages minutes after Ronald Reagan is inaugurated as US President.
01-21: 1793: King Louis XVI of France is executed by guillotine in Paris during the French Revolution.
01-22: 1901: Queen Victoria of the United Kingdom dies after a historic reign of over 63 years.
01-23: 1960: The bathyscaphe Trieste reaches a record depth of 10,911 meters at the bottom of the Mariana Trench.
01-24: 1984: Apple Computer introduces the Macintosh personal computer during a Super Bowl commercial.
01-25: 1915: Alexander Graham Bell and Thomas A. Watson conduct the first transcontinental telephone call.
01-26: 1788: The British First Fleet lands at Port Jackson, establishing Sydney and founding modern Australia.
01-27: 1945: The Soviet Red Army liberates the Auschwitz-Birkenau concentration camp in occupied Poland.
01-28: 1986: The Space Shuttle Challenger disintegrates 73 seconds after launch, killing all seven crew members.
01-29: 1886: Karl Benz files a patent for the Benz Patent-Motorwagen, the first modern automobile.
01-30: 1948: Indian political and spiritual leader Mahatma Gandhi is assassinated in New Delhi.
01-31: 1958: The United States successfully launches Explorer 1, its first artificial Earth satellite.
02-01: 2003: Space Shuttle Columbia disintegrates during re-entry into Earth's atmosphere, killing all seven astronauts.
02-02: 1943: The Battle of Stalingrad ends with the unconditional surrender of the German 6th Army.
02-03: 1966: The Soviet Union's Luna 9 becomes the first spacecraft to achieve a soft landing on the Moon.
02-04: 2004: Mark Zuckerberg launches the social networking website Facebook from his Harvard University dorm room.
02-05: 1919: United Artists film studio is incorporated by Charlie Chaplin, Mary Pickford, Douglas Fairbanks, and D.W. Griffith.
02-06: 1952: Princess Elizabeth ascends to the throne as Queen Elizabeth II following the death of King George VI.
02-07: 1992: The Maastricht Treaty is signed by members of the European Communities, creating the European Union.
02-08: 1904: The Russo-Japanese War begins with a surprise Japanese torpedo boat attack at Port Arthur.
02-09: 1964: The Beatles make their iconic first appearance on The Ed Sullivan Show, attracting record viewership.
02-10: 1996: IBM's Deep Blue chess-playing supercomputer defeats World Champion Garry Kasparov for the first time.
02-11: 1990: South African anti-apartheid activist Nelson Mandela is released from prison after 27 years.
02-12: 1809: Abraham Lincoln, 16th US President, and Charles Darwin, English naturalist, are both born.
02-13: 1945: The Allied aerial bombing of the German city of Dresden begins, causing catastrophic destruction.
02-14: 2005: The video-sharing platform YouTube is founded by Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim.
02-15: 2013: A meteor explodes over the city of Chelyabinsk, Russia, injuring over 1,500 people.
02-16: 1923: English archaeologist Howard Carter officially opens the burial chamber of Pharaoh Tutankhamun.
02-17: 2008: Kosovo officially declares its independence from Serbia.
02-18: 1930: American astronomer Clyde Tombaugh discovers the dwarf planet Pluto while studying photographs.
02-19: 1942: US President Franklin D. Roosevelt signs Executive Order 9066, leading to the internment of Japanese Americans.
02-20: 1962: John Glenn becomes the first American to orbit the Earth aboard the Friendship 7 spacecraft.
02-21: 1848: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels publish The Communist Manifesto in London.
02-22: 1997: Scientists in Scotland announce the successful cloning of Dolly the sheep, the first cloned mammal.
02-23: 1945: US Marines raise the American flag atop Mount Suribachi during the Battle of Iwo Jima.
02-24: 2022: Russia launches a full-scale military invasion of Ukraine, escalating the ongoing conflict.
02-25: 1956: Nikita Khrushchev delivers his Secret Speech denouncing the cult of personality of Joseph Stalin.
02-26: 1993: A truck bomb explodes below the North Tower of the World Trade Center in New York City.
02-27: 1933: The Reichstag building in Berlin is set on fire, an event used by Nazis to suspend civil liberties.
02-28: 1953: James Watson and Francis Crick announce they have discovered the double-helix structure of DNA.
02-29: 1940: Hattie McDaniel becomes the first African American to win an Academy Award.
03-01: 1954: The United States detonates Castle Bravo, its most powerful thermonuclear weapon, at Bikini Atoll.
03-02: 1969: The Anglo-French supersonic airliner Concorde makes its first successful test flight.
03-03: 1918: Soviet Russia signs the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk, ending its participation in World War I.
03-04: 1789: The Constitution of the United States goes into effect as the first Federal Congress meets.
03-05: 1946: Winston Churchill delivers his famous 'Iron Curtain' speech at Westminster College in Missouri.
03-06: 1869: Dmitri Mendeleev presents the first periodic table of chemical elements to the Russian Chemical Society.
03-07: 1876: Alexander Graham Bell is granted a patent for his invention of the telephone.
03-08: 1917: The February Revolution begins in Russia, leading to the abdication of Tsar Nicholas II.
03-09: 1959: The iconic Barbie doll makes its debut at the American International Toy Fair in New York.
03-10: 1876: Alexander Graham Bell makes the first successful telephone call, speaking to Thomas Watson.
03-11: 2011: A massive 9.0 magnitude earthquake and tsunami strike Japan, triggering the Fukushima nuclear disaster.
03-12: 1930: Mahatma Gandhi begins the Salt March, a 24-day nonviolent protest against British rule.
03-13: 1781: British astronomer William Herschel discovers the planet Uranus from his garden.
03-14: 1879: Theoretical physicist Albert Einstein is born in Ulm, Germany.
03-15: 44 BC: Roman dictator Julius Caesar is assassinated by a group of senators on the Ides of March.
03-16: 1926: Robert Goddard launches the world's first liquid-fueled rocket in Auburn, Massachusetts.
03-17: 1861: The Kingdom of Italy is officially proclaimed, unifying most of the Italian peninsula.
03-18: 1965: Soviet cosmonaut Alexei Leonov becomes the first person to conduct a spacewalk.
03-19: 1918: The United States Congress establishes standard time zones and approves daylight saving time.
03-20: 2003: A United States-led coalition invades Iraq, marking the beginning of the Iraq War.
03-21: 1960: South African police open fire on peaceful anti-apartheid demonstrators in the Sharpeville massacre.
03-22: 1945: The Arab League is formally established in Cairo, Egypt, by six founding member states.
03-23: 1983: US President Ronald Reagan announces the Strategic Defense Initiative, nicknamed Star Wars.
03-24: 1989: The Exxon Valdez oil tanker runs aground in Alaska, causing a catastrophic environmental disaster.
03-25: 1957: The Treaty of Rome is signed, establishing the European Economic Community.
03-26: 1979: Anwar Sadat and Menachem Begin sign the Egypt-Israel peace treaty at the White House.
03-27: 1977: Two Boeing 747s collide on a runway in Tenerife, resulting in the deadliest aviation accident in history.
03-28: 1979: A partial meltdown occurs at the Three Mile Island nuclear generating station in Pennsylvania.
03-29: 1974: The ancient Terracotta Army is discovered by local farmers in Xi'an, China.
03-30: 1867: US Secretary of State William H. Seward signs an agreement to purchase Alaska from Russia.
03-31: 1889: The Eiffel Tower is officially inaugurated in Paris, France, celebrating the World's Fair.
04-01: 1976: Apple Computer Company is founded by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne.
04-02: 1982: Argentine special forces invade the Falkland Islands, triggering the Falklands War.
04-03: 1973: Martin Cooper of Motorola makes the first handheld cellular phone call in New York City.
04-04: 1968: American civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr. is assassinated in Memphis, Tennessee.
04-05: 1994: Kurt Cobain, lead singer and guitarist of the grunge band Nirvana, dies by suicide.
04-06: 1896: The opening ceremony of the first modern Olympic Games is celebrated in Athens, Greece.
04-07: 1948: The World Health Organization is established by the United Nations to manage global health.
04-08: 1973: Renowned Spanish cubist artist and painter Pablo Picasso dies in Mougins, France.
04-09: 1865: Confederate General Robert E. Lee surrenders to Union General Ulysses S. Grant, ending the American Civil War.
04-10: 1912: The luxurious British passenger liner RMS Titanic sets sail from Southampton on its maiden voyage.
04-11: 1970: Apollo 13 is launched towards the Moon, later suffering a severe mid-mission explosion.
04-12: 1961: Yuri Gagarin becomes the first human to travel into space.
04-13: 1943: Nazi Germany announces the discovery of mass graves of executed Polish officers in the Katyń forest.
04-14: 1865: US President Abraham Lincoln is shot by John Wilkes Booth while attending a play at Ford's Theatre.
04-15: 1912: The RMS Titanic sinks into the Atlantic Ocean after striking an iceberg, killing over 1,500 people.
04-16: 1947: American financier Bernard Baruch popularizes the term 'Cold War' during a speech.
04-17: 1961: A group of CIA-sponsored Cuban exiles launches the unsuccessful Bay of Pigs Invasion.
04-18: 1906: A major earthquake and devastating fires strike San Francisco, destroying most of the city.
04-19: 1995: The Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building in Oklahoma City is destroyed by a domestic terrorist truck bomb.
04-20: 1999: Two students perpetrate the Columbine High School massacre in Colorado, killing 13 people.
04-21: 753 BC: Romulus and Remus officially found the city of Rome, according to ancient Roman legend.
04-22: 1970: The first Earth Day is celebrated by millions of people across the United States to promote environmentalism.
04-23: 1564: Standard celebrated birth date of legendary English playwright and poet William Shakespeare.
04-24: 1990: The Hubble Space Telescope is successfully launched into orbit by the Space Shuttle Discovery.
04-25: 1953: Francis Crick and James Watson publish their seminal paper on the double-helix structure of DNA.
04-26: 1986: A catastrophic explosion and nuclear meltdown occur at the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant in Ukraine.
04-27: 1994: South Africa holds its first fully multiracial democratic election, electing Nelson Mandela.
04-28: 1945: Italian fascist dictator Benito Mussolini is executed by Italian partisans while attempting to escape.
04-29: 1992: Major civil unrest breaks out in Los Angeles following the acquittal of officers in the Rodney King trial.
04-30: 1945: Nazi dictator Adolf Hitler commits suicide alongside Eva Braun in his underground bunker in Berlin.
05-01: 1931: The Empire State Building is officially opened in New York City.
05-02: 2011: Osama bin Laden is killed by US special forces during a raid in Abbottabad, Pakistan.
05-03: 1978: The first unsolicited commercial email (spam) is sent over ARPANET.
05-04: 1979: Margaret Thatcher becomes the first female Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.
05-05: 1821: French Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte dies in exile on the island of Saint Helena.
05-06: 1937: The German airship Hindenburg catches fire and is destroyed while docking in New Jersey.
05-07: 1915: The British ocean liner RMS Lusitania is torpedoed and sunk by a German U-boat.
05-08: 1945: Nazi Germany unconditionally surrenders, marking Victory in Europe (VE) Day.
05-09: 1950: French Foreign Minister Robert Schuman presents the Schuman Declaration, laying the foundation for the EU.
05-10: 1994: Nelson Mandela is inaugurated as South Africa's first Black president.
05-11: 1997: Deep Blue defeats Garry Kasparov in a six-game match, the first time a computer beats a world champion under tournament conditions.
05-12: 2008: A catastrophic 8.0 magnitude earthquake strikes Sichuan, China, killing tens of thousands.
05-13: 1981: Pope John Paul II is shot and critically wounded by Mehmet Ali Ağca in St. Peter's Square.
05-14: 1948: David Ben-Gurion reads the Declaration of Independence, establishing the State of Israel.
05-15: 1940: McDonald's opens its first restaurant in San Bernardino, California.
05-16: 1929: The first Academy Awards ceremony is held at the Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel in Los Angeles.
05-17: 1954: The US Supreme Court rules unanimously in Brown v. Board of Education that racial segregation in public schools is unconstitutional.
05-18: 1980: Mount St. Helens erupts in Washington state, causing massive destruction and killing 57 people.
05-19: 1536: Anne Boleyn, the second wife of King Henry VIII of England, is executed by beheading.
05-20: 1927: Charles Lindbergh takes off from New York on the first solo non-stop transatlantic flight.
05-21: 1927: Charles Lindbergh lands in Paris, completing his historic transatlantic flight.
05-22: 1990: North Yemen and South Yemen merge to form the unified Republic of Yemen.
05-23: 1934: Notorious American bank robbers Bonnie and Clyde are ambushed and killed by police in Louisiana.
05-24: 1883: The Brooklyn Bridge over the East River is opened to traffic after 14 years of construction.
05-25: 1961: US President John F. Kennedy announces the goal of landing a man on the Moon before the decade is out.
05-26: 1897: Irish author Bram Stoker's gothic novel 'Dracula' is first published in London.
05-27: 1937: The Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco is officially opened to pedestrians.
05-28: 1940: The Dunkirk evacuation begins, rescuing over 300,000 Allied soldiers from France.
05-29: 1953: Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay become the first to reach the summit of Mount Everest.
05-30: 1431: Joan of Arc is burned at the stake by the English in Rouen, France, during the Hundred Years' War.
05-31: 1911: The RMS Titanic is launched from the Harland and Wolff shipyard in Belfast.
06-01: 1980: Cable News Network (CNN), the first 24-hour television news channel, begins broadcasting.
06-02: 1953: The coronation of Queen Elizabeth II takes place at Westminster Abbey in London.
06-03: 1965: Edward White becomes the first American to conduct a spacewalk, during the Gemini 4 mission.
06-04: 1989: The Chinese military violently clears pro-democracy protestors from Tiananmen Square in Beijing.
06-05: 1981: The US CDC reports the first clinical cases of what would later become known as AIDS.
06-06: 1944: The D-Day invasion begins as Allied forces land on the beaches of Normandy, France.
06-07: 1929: The Lateran Treaty is ratified, establishing Vatican City as an independent sovereign state.
06-08: 1949: George Orwell's dystopian novel 'Nineteen Eighty-Four' is published in London.
06-09: 1934: Donald Duck makes his first-ever appearance in the Disney cartoon 'The Wise Little Hen'.
06-10: 1935: Alcoholics Anonymous (AA) is founded in Akron, Ohio, by Bill Wilson and Dr. Bob Smith.
06-11: 1963: Buddhist monk Thich Quang Duc self-immolates in Saigon to protest religious persecution.
06-12: 1987: US President Ronald Reagan gives his famous speech at the Berlin Wall, demanding 'Tear down this wall!'.
06-13: 1971: The New York Times begins publishing the Pentagon Papers, revealing secret US history in Vietnam.
06-14: 1777: The Continental Congress adopts the Stars and Stripes as the official flag of the United States.
06-15: 1215: King John of England signs the Magna Carta at Runnymede, limiting royal power.
06-16: 1963: Soviet cosmonaut Valentina Tereshkova becomes the first woman in space aboard Vostok 6.
06-17: 1972: Five men are arrested for breaking into the Democratic National Committee headquarters, initiating the Watergate scandal.
06-18: 1815: Napoleon Bonaparte is decisively defeated at the Battle of Waterloo, ending the Napoleonic Wars.
06-19: 1865: Union soldiers arrive in Galveston, Texas, enforcing the Emancipation Proclamation and giving birth to Juneteenth.
06-20: 1975: The thriller movie 'Jaws' is released in theaters, pioneering the modern summer blockbuster.
06-21: 2004: SpaceShipOne becomes the first privately funded spacecraft to achieve human spaceflight.
06-22: 1941: Nazi Germany launches Operation Barbarossa, a massive surprise invasion of the Soviet Union.
06-23: 2016: The United Kingdom votes in a referendum to leave the European Union, an event known as Brexit.
06-24: 1948: The Soviet Union begins the Berlin Blockade, cutting off ground access to West Berlin.
06-25: 1998: Microsoft releases the Windows 98 operating system.
06-26: 1945: The United Nations Charter is signed by delegates from 50 nations in San Francisco.
06-27: 1954: The Obninsk Nuclear Power Plant becomes the world's first grid-connected nuclear power station.
06-28: 1914: Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria is assassinated in Sarajevo, triggering World War I.
06-29: 2007: Apple releases the first-generation iPhone in the United States.
06-30: 1908: A massive, mysterious explosion occurs near the Podkamennaya Tunguska River in Siberia.
07-01: 1997: Hong Kong is officially handed over to China by the United Kingdom, ending 156 years of British rule.
07-02: 1964: US President Lyndon B. Johnson signs the landmark Civil Rights Act of 1964 into law.
07-03: 1886: Karl Benz officially drives the Benz Patent-Motorwagen, the first automobile, in Mannheim, Germany.
07-04: 1776: The United States Declaration of Independence is adopted by the Second Continental Congress.
07-05: 1996: Dolly the sheep, the first mammal to be successfully cloned from an adult cell, is born in Scotland.
07-06: 1885: Louis Pasteur successfully tests his rabies vaccine on Joseph Meister, a boy bitten by a rabid dog.
07-07: 1937: The Marco Polo Bridge Incident triggers the full-scale outbreak of the Second Sino-Japanese War.
07-08: 1947: The Roswell Army Air Field issues a press release about a 'flying disc,' sparking the Roswell UFO incident.
07-09: 1816: Argentina declares its formal independence from the Spanish Empire.
07-10: 1940: The Battle of Britain begins as the German Luftwaffe launches massive air raids against the UK.
07-11: 1995: The Srebrenica massacre begins during the Bosnian War, resulting in the deaths of over 8,000 Bosniaks.
07-12: 1962: The Rolling Stones perform their first official concert at the Marquee Club in London.
07-13: 1985: The dual-venue Live Aid benefit concerts are held in London and Philadelphia to raise funds for Ethiopian famine relief.
07-14: 1789: Citizens of Paris storm the Bastille prison, a pivotal flashpoint of the French Revolution.
07-15: 2006: The social media platform Twitter is officially launched to the public.
07-16: 1945: The United States detonates the world's first nuclear weapon in the Trinity test in New Mexico.
07-17: 1918: Tsar Nicholas II of Russia and his immediate family are executed by Bolshevik forces in Yekaterinburg.
07-18: 1925: Adolf Hitler publishes his personal manifesto and political tract, Mein Kampf.
07-19: 1900: The first line of the Paris Métro subway system is opened during the World's Fair.
07-20: 1969: Apollo 11 lands on the Moon; Neil Armstrong takes his first step.
07-21: 1954: The Geneva Accords formally partition Vietnam into North Vietnam and South Vietnam.
07-22: 2011: Anders Behring Breivik kills 77 people in twin terrorist attacks in Oslo and Utøya, Norway.
07-23: 1914: Austria-Hungary issues a harsh ultimatum to Serbia following the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.
07-24: 1911: American explorer Hiram Bingham re-discovers the lost Inca citadel of Machu Picchu in Peru.
07-25: 1978: Louise Brown, the world's first human baby conceived through in vitro fertilization (IVF), is born in England.
07-26: 1956: Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser nationalizes the Suez Canal, triggering the Suez Crisis.
07-27: 1953: The Korean Armistice Agreement is signed, halting active hostilities in the Korean War.
07-28: 1914: Austria-Hungary declares war on Serbia, officially marking the start of World War I.
07-29: 1958: US President Dwight D. Eisenhower signs the National Aeronautics and Space Act, creating NASA.
07-30: 1965: US President Lyndon B. Johnson signs the Social Security Amendments, establishing Medicare and Medicaid.
07-31: 1971: Apollo 15 astronauts drive the first Lunar Roving Vehicle on the surface of the Moon.
08-01: 1914: The German Empire declares war on the Russian Empire at the onset of World War I.
08-02: 1990: Iraq invades Kuwait, leading directly to the outbreak of the Persian Gulf War.
08-03: 1492: Christopher Columbus sets sail from Palos de la Frontera, Spain, on his first voyage across the Atlantic.
08-04: 1944: Anne Frank and her family are discovered and arrested by the Gestapo in their Amsterdam hiding place.
08-05: 2012: NASA's Curiosity rover successfully lands on the surface of Mars to study its climate and geology.
08-06: 1991: Tim Berners-Lee publishes the first-ever website on the World Wide Web.
08-07: 1998: The US Embassies in Nairobi, Kenya, and Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, are bombed by al-Qaeda.
08-08: 1974: US President Richard Nixon announces his resignation on television due to the Watergate scandal.
08-09: 1945: The United States drops an atomic bomb code-named 'Fat Man' on the Japanese city of Nagasaki.
08-10: 1519: Ferdinand Magellan's five ships set sail from Seville, Spain, to circumnavigate the globe.
08-11: 1973: DJ Kool Herc pioneers hip-hop music at a back-to-school party in the Bronx, New York.
08-12: 1953: The Soviet Union conducts its first successful test of a thermonuclear weapon (hydrogen bomb).
08-13: 1961: East Germany begins constructing the Berlin Wall to prevent citizens from fleeing to the West.
08-14: 1945: Japan accepts the Potsdam Declaration, unconditionally surrendering and ending World War II.
08-15: 1969: The legendary Woodstock Music & Art Fair opens in Bethel, New York, drawing over 400,000 people.
08-16: 1977: Elvis Presley, the American singer widely known as the 'King of Rock and Roll,' dies at age 42.
08-17: 1999: A catastrophic 7.6 magnitude earthquake strikes northwestern Turkey, killing over 17,000 people.
08-18: 1920: The 19th Amendment to the US Constitution is ratified, granting women the right to vote.
08-19: 1991: Soviet hardliners launch a failed coup attempt against President Mikhail Gorbachev.
08-20: 1940: Exiled Russian revolutionary Leon Trotsky is fatally attacked with an ice axe in Mexico.
08-21: 1911: The Mona Lisa painting by Leonardo da Vinci is stolen from the Louvre Museum in Paris.
08-22: 2004: Edvard Munch's famous paintings 'The Scream' and 'Madonna' are stolen from the Munch Museum in Oslo.
08-23: 1939: Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union sign the Molotov-Ribbentrop non-aggression pact.
08-24: 79 AD: Mount Vesuvius erupts, destroying the ancient Roman cities of Pompeii and Herculaneum.
08-25: 1944: Allied forces officially liberate Paris from Nazi military occupation during World War II.
08-26: 1789: The National Constituent Assembly of France approves the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen.
08-27: 1883: The eruption of the Krakatoa volcano in Indonesia reaches its violent, catastrophic climax.
08-28: 1963: Martin Luther King Jr. delivers his iconic 'I Have a Dream' speech at the March on Washington.
08-29: 1949: The Soviet Union successfully tests its first atomic bomb at the Semipalatinsk test site.
08-30: 1963: The Hotline between Washington and Moscow (the 'Red Telephone') goes into official operation.
08-31: 1997: Diana, Princess of Wales, dies following a high-speed car crash in a Paris tunnel.
09-01: 1939: World War II officially begins following the invasion of Poland.
09-02: 1945: Japan signs the formal instrument of surrender aboard the USS Missouri, officially ending World War II.
09-03: 1783: The Treaty of Paris is signed, formally ending the American Revolutionary War.
09-04: 1998: Google is founded by Larry Page and Sergey Brin while they are students at Stanford University.
09-05: 1977: NASA launches the Voyager 1 spacecraft to study the outer Solar System and interstellar space.
09-06: 1997: The funeral of Diana, Princess of Wales, is held at Westminster Abbey, watched by millions worldwide.
09-07: 1822: Brazil declares its official independence from the Kingdom of Portugal.
09-08: 1565: St. Augustine, Florida, the oldest continuously occupied European settlement in the continental US, is founded.
09-09: 1976: Mao Zedong, the founding father of the People's Republic of China, dies at age 82.
09-10: 2008: The Large Hadron Collider at CERN, the world's largest particle accelerator, is first powered up.
09-11: 2001: Al-Qaeda terrorists hijack four passenger planes, executing the September 11 terrorist attacks on the US.
09-12: 1962: US President John F. Kennedy delivers his famous 'We choose to go to the Moon' speech at Rice University.
09-13: 1993: The Oslo I Accord is signed by PLO leader Yasser Arafat and Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin.
09-14: 1812: The Fire of Moscow begins as Russian troops and residents abandon the city ahead of Napoleon's advancing army.
09-15: 2008: Lehman Brothers files for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection, triggering the global financial crisis.
09-16: 1975: Papua New Guinea gains full independence from the Commonwealth of Australia.
09-17: 1787: The United States Constitution is signed by delegates at the Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia.
09-18: 1947: The National Security Act of 1947 takes effect, establishing the United States Air Force and the CIA.
09-19: 1985: A massive 8.0 magnitude earthquake strikes Mexico City, killing thousands and causing catastrophic damage.
09-20: 2011: The United States military officially ends its 'Don't Ask, Don't Tell' policy regarding LGBTQ+ service members.
09-21: 1937: J.R.R. Tolkien's classic high-fantasy novel 'The Hobbit' is first published by Allen & Unwin.
09-22: 1862: US President Abraham Lincoln issues the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation during the Civil War.
09-23: 1846: The planet Neptune is discovered by astronomer Johann Gottfried Galle based on mathematical predictions.
09-24: 1948: The Honda Motor Company is formally incorporated in Japan by Soichiro Honda and Takeo Fujisawa.
09-25: 1981: Sandra Day O'Connor is sworn in as the first female Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States.
09-26: 1960: The first televised US presidential debate takes place between John F. Kennedy and Richard Nixon.
09-27: 1905: Albert Einstein publishes his paper introducing the mass-energy equivalence equation, E=mc².
09-28: 1928: Scottish scientist Alexander Fleming discovers penicillin, the world's first effective antibiotic.
09-29: 1954: The European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN) is officially established in Geneva.
09-30: 1954: The USS Nautilus, the world's first operational nuclear-powered submarine, is commissioned.
10-01: 1949: The People's Republic of China is formally proclaimed by Mao Zedong in Beijing's Tiananmen Square.
10-02: 1869: Indian political and spiritual leader Mahatma Gandhi is born in Porbandar, India.
10-03: 1990: East Germany and West Germany are officially reunited, dissolving the German Democratic Republic.
10-04: 1957: Sputnik 1, the first artificial Earth satellite, is successfully launched.
10-05: 1962: The first James Bond film, 'Dr. No', premieres in London theaters.
10-06: 1973: Egypt and Syria launch a coordinated surprise attack on Israel, initiating the Yom Kippur War.
10-07: 2001: The United States and its allies launch military operations in Afghanistan, starting the War in Afghanistan.
10-08: 1871: The Great Chicago Fire breaks out, destroying roughly four square miles of the city.
10-09: 1967: Marxist revolutionary Che Guevara is executed in Bolivia one day after his capture by military forces.
10-10: 1973: US Vice President Spiro Agnew resigns after being charged with federal income tax evasion.
10-11: 1962: Pope John XXIII convenes the Second Vatican Council, modernizing the Catholic Church.
10-12: 1492: Christopher Columbus's expedition makes landfall in the Caribbean, marking the European discovery of the Americas.
10-13: 2010: The Copiapó mining accident in Chile ends as all 33 trapped miners are successfully rescued after 69 days.
10-14: 1947: American test pilot Chuck Yeager breaks the sound barrier for the first time in the Bell X-1.
10-15: 1997: The Cassini-Huygens spacecraft is launched from Cape Canaveral to study Saturn and its moons.
10-16: 1962: The Cuban Missile Crisis begins as US President John F. Kennedy is shown photos of Soviet missiles in Cuba.
10-17: 1973: OPEC begins an oil embargo against Western nations supporting Israel in the Yom Kippur War.
10-18: 1922: The British Broadcasting Company (BBC) is officially founded in London.
10-19: 1987: Stock markets around the world crash on 'Black Monday', with the Dow Jones dropping over 22 percent.
10-20: 1973: US President Richard Nixon orders the firing of Watergate Prosecutor Archibald Cox in the 'Saturday Night Massacre'.
10-21: 1805: Admiral Lord Nelson defeats the combined French and Spanish fleets at the Battle of Trafalgar.
10-22: 1962: US President John F. Kennedy announces a naval blockade of Cuba on television during the Cuban Missile Crisis.
10-23: 2001: Apple introduces the original iPod, changing the landscape of digital music portable players.
10-24: 1945: The United Nations Charter takes effect, officially establishing the international organization.
10-25: 1415: Henry V leads the English army to a decisive victory against the French at the Battle of Agincourt.
10-26: 2001: US President George W. Bush signs the USA PATRIOT Act into law following the September 11 attacks.
10-27: 1962: A Soviet submarine nearly fires a nuclear torpedo during the Cuban Missile Crisis, averted by Vasili Arkhipov.
10-28: 1886: The Statue of Liberty is officially dedicated in New York Harbor by US President Grover Cleveland.
10-29: 1929: The US stock market crashes completely on 'Black Tuesday', triggering the Great Depression.
10-30: 1938: Orson Welles broadcasts his radio adaptation of 'The War of the Worlds', causing widespread panic.
10-31: 1517: Martin Luther nails his Ninety-five Theses to the door of Wittenberg Church, starting the Protestant Reformation.
11-01: 1512: The ceiling of the Sistine Chapel, painted by Michelangelo, is exhibited to the public for the first time.
11-02: 1917: British Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour issues the Balfour Declaration, supporting a Jewish homeland in Palestine.
11-03: 1957: The Soviet Union launches Sputnik 2 carrying Laika the dog, the first living creature in Earth orbit.
11-04: 1922: British archaeologist Howard Carter discovers the entrance to King Tutankhamun's tomb in Egypt.
11-05: 1605: The Gunpowder Plot is foiled in England when Guy Fawkes is arrested beneath the House of Lords.
11-06: 1860: Abraham Lincoln is elected as the 16th President of the United States, later leading to the Civil War.
11-07: 1917: The Bolsheviks launch an armed insurrection in Petrograd, initiating the October Revolution in Russia.
11-08: 1923: Adolf Hitler launches the failed Beer Hall Putsch coup attempt in Munich, Germany.
11-09: 1989: The fall of the Berlin Wall begins, reuniting East and West Germany.
11-10: 1975: The United Nations General Assembly passes Resolution 3379, declaring Zionism a form of racism.
11-11: 1918: An armistice is signed between the Allies and Germany, ending active fighting in World War I.
11-12: 1970: The Bhola cyclone strikes East Pakistan (now Bangladesh), killing an estimated 300,000 to 500,000 people.
11-13: 2015: Coordinated terrorist attacks by ISIS in Paris, France, target the Bataclan and other sites, killing 130 people.
11-14: 1969: NASA launches Apollo 12, the second manned mission to land on the Moon.
11-15: 1971: Intel introduces the world's first single-chip commercial microprocessor, the Intel 4004.
11-16: 1532: Spanish conquistador Francisco Pizarro captures Incan Emperor Atahualpa at the Battle of Cajamarca.
11-17: 1869: The Suez Canal in Egypt is officially opened, connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.
11-18: 1978: Over 900 members of the Peoples Temple cult die in a mass suicide-murder in Jonestown, Guyana.
11-19: 1863: US President Abraham Lincoln delivers the Gettysburg Address at the dedication of the Soldiers' National Cemetery.
11-20: 1945: The Nuremberg trials begin in Germany against 24 major Nazi political and military leaders.
11-21: 1783: Pilâtre de Rozier and Marquis d'Arlandes make the first untethered hot air balloon flight in Paris.
11-22: 1963: US President John F. Kennedy is assassinated by Lee Harvey Oswald in Dallas, Texas.
11-23: 1936: The first issue of Life magazine, featuring a cover photo of the Fort Peck Dam, is published.
11-24: 1859: English naturalist Charles Darwin publishes his seminal scientific work, On the Origin of Species.
11-25: 1915: Albert Einstein presents his field equations of general relativity to the Prussian Academy of Sciences.
11-26: 1922: Howard Carter and Lord Carnarvon enter the tomb of Pharaoh Tutankhamun for the first time.
11-27: 2005: The first partial human face transplant is successfully performed on Isabelle Dinoire in Amiens, France.
11-28: 1520: Ferdinand Magellan's fleet reaches the Pacific Ocean, becoming the first Europeans to sail there from the Atlantic.
11-29: 1947: The United Nations General Assembly approves the Partition Plan for Palestine via Resolution 181.
11-30: 1954: The Hodges meteorite falls in Sylacauga, Alabama, becoming the first documented object to injure a human.
12-01: 1955: Rosa Parks refuses to give up her bus seat to a white man in Montgomery, Alabama, sparking civil rights protests.
12-02: 1804: Napoleon Bonaparte is crowned Emperor of the French at Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris.
12-03: 1967: Dr. Christiaan Barnard performs the world's first successful human-to-human heart transplant in South Africa.
12-04: 1996: NASA launches the Mars Pathfinder spacecraft carrying the Sojourner rover toward Mars.
12-05: 1933: The 21st Amendment to the US Constitution is ratified, officially repealing Prohibition.
12-06: 1917: The Halifax Explosion occurs in Canada when a munitions ship collides with another vessel, killing over 1,900 people.
12-07: 1941: The Imperial Japanese Navy launches a surprise military strike on the United States naval base at Pearl Harbor.
12-08: 1980: Former Beatles member John Lennon is shot and killed by Mark David Chapman outside his New York apartment.
12-09: 1946: The Nuremberg Doctors' Trial begins, prosecuting Nazi physicians involved in human experimentation.
12-10: 1948: The United Nations General Assembly adopts the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.
12-11: 2001: The People's Republic of China officially joins the World Trade Organization (WTO).
12-12: 1901: Guglielmo Marconi receives the first transatlantic radio signal, sent from Cornwall to Newfoundland.
12-13: 2003: Deposed Iraqi President Saddam Hussein is captured by US military forces during Operation Red Dawn.
12-14: 1911: Roald Amundsen's Norwegian expedition becomes the first to successfully reach the South Pole.
12-15: 1791: The United States Bill of Rights officially becomes law following ratification by the state of Virginia.
12-16: 1773: Members of the Sons of Liberty dump tea into Boston Harbor during the Boston Tea Party protest.
12-17: 1903: The Wright brothers make the first controlled, sustained flight of a powered, heavier-than-air aircraft.
12-18: 1916: The Battle of Verdun ends with French forces successfully repelling the German army after ten months.
12-19: 1998: The US House of Representatives approves articles of impeachment against President Bill Clinton.
12-20: 1999: Macau is officially handed over to the People's Republic of China by Portugal, ending colonial rule.
12-21: 1988: Pan Am Flight 103 explodes over Lockerbie, Scotland, due to a terrorist bomb, killing 270 people.
12-22: 1989: The Romanian Revolution concludes as communist dictator Nicolae Ceaușescu is overthrown.
12-23: 1913: The Federal Reserve System is created as US President Woodrow Wilson signs the Federal Reserve Act.
12-24: 1968: The crew of Apollo 8 enters orbit around the Moon, becoming the first humans to witness Earthrise.
12-25: 2021: The James Webb Space Telescope is successfully launched into space.
12-26: 2004: A massive 9.1 magnitude earthquake triggers a devastating Indian Ocean tsunami, killing over 230,000 people.
12-27: 2007: Former Prime Minister of Pakistan Benazir Bhutto is assassinated in a suicide attack in Rawalpindi.
12-28: 1895: The Lumière brothers hold their first commercial public screening of cinematographic films in Paris.
12-29: 1890: The US Army kills over 150 Lakota Sioux people in the tragic Wounded Knee Massacre in South Dakota.
12-30: 2006: Former Iraqi President Saddam Hussein is executed by hanging in Baghdad.
12-31: 1999: Russian President Boris Yeltsin resigns, naming Vladimir Putin as acting president.
